name: Glassware, stacked, in the Josef Hoffmann exhibit at the MAK
description:
Josef Hoffmann was an Architecht and Designer (1870-1956). He was a cofounder of the Vienna Secession and the Vienna Werkstätte.

There is an exhibit of his work at the MAK http://www.mak.at/mak_design_labor/hoffmann_geometrisch

If I understood correctly, they had a visiting artist who made some geometric constructions, like this one, using ceramics and glassware from the MAK collection.

The constructions pull the eye in.

description: From wikipedia "Theseus Fighting the Centaur (1804–1819), Kunsthistorisches Museum, Vienna. Napoleon ordered it for the Corso in Milan; Emperor Franz I bought it for the Theseus Temple in the Volksgarten in Vienna; moved to Kunsthistorisches Museum in 1891."

name: Equestrian Statue of Marcus Aurelius
description:
This is the statue which stood in the Campidoglio in Rome, the plaza on the top of the Capitoline hill. The Capitolini Museums where designed by Michealangelo specifically to showcase this sculpture. And Michaelangelo designed the original base.

A few years ago conservation issues took the front, and after much discussion the original was restored and a new area was constructed in the museum to display it, and a reproduction was placed in the square.

The statue was first erected in 175 CE...so, pretty old...

name: Three Sound Artists...
description:
Drei Klangkünstler die keine Worte verlieren, Reibungspunkte und Gemeinsamkeiten suchen, Musik finden und Klang werdende Gedanken und Emotionen jenen zurückgeben, die dieses Gefühl wieder haben wollen, sich verlieren zu können. 

(loosely translated...Three sound artists lose no words, search frictions and similarities, find music and sound expectant thoughts and emotions return those who want to have that feeling again, to lose himself.)

Lukas Lauermann _ Cello
Stefan Sterzinger _ Akkordeon
Raphael Sas _ Gitarre

Detail of a decorative light fixure on the Maria Theresien Platz. This is the large square between the Natural History and Art History Museums in Vienna.

I have been noticing the metal work, and how the metal work is attached to the stone. These light fixtures are are all through the park. The statue of Maria Theresia and the dome of the Natural History museum are in the background. (The grey area on the left covers up a missing images).

name: Bulletin Board at Sasha Shulgin's lab (revised)
description:
(slightly cleaned up version of http://gigapan.com/gigapans/164466)
In June I was given the opportunity to take Gigapans in Sasha Shulgin's lab. This is the Bulletin Board in his lab.

Taken June 10, 2014.
The lab is a fascinating place! Basically a garden shed in the back of the property, but a garden shed where he created over 200 psycho pharmaceutical substances. Many of which have shown incredible promise in treating chronic conditions, such as PTSD.

name: A gypsy wagon...
description:
Or not really, but with a similar design. These wagons are all over, wherever there is a city work site. 2/3'rds of the wagon is a worker break and office area, I think I saw a little stove in one for heating, and the rest is tool and supply storage.

And the area to the lower right is Photoshop content aware fill - isn't technology magic?

name: Wehrmann in Eisen - The Iron Soldier
description:
This statue was first put up in 1915 in Vienna. For a donation people could drive a nail into the statue. The donations supported WWI widows and orphans. It is said that 500,000 iron nails were driven into the wooden figure.

(given Gigapan technology I would expect each one to be snapshotted and labeled :-)

There is more history, read the description (in the image)

name: Willow Brugh
description:
I had the chance to visit and to take some portraits of my friend Willow. I like how this picture seems to recreate all the bad photo tropes of an Eastern European passport photo :-) She was lit from a bank of windows with northern exposure on her left side (right side in the photo) and by a flash with an umbrella on the right side. You can see the umbrella reflected in her eyes.

I need to try setting up a large light source, like a whole white wall, so that the specular highlights are not visible. OTOH, it is fun to see what gets reflected.

name: South side of SP(G10)
description:
I had this labeled as the North Side of SP Crater in my notes, but clearly this is the south side. Whoops. I think that points to some of the issues inherent in doing field work.


I took notes on my netbook on every shot, and I made a waypoint. Using those data sources, plus sanity checking in Google Earth, provides some hope that the information is really correct. And yet, errors slip through :-/


Last shot of the day. My notes say 'We're losing light, I'm not that optimistic about it.' But I'm happy with the light, with the sense of quiet mass as the day passes and the volcanos remain.


After this we packed up the car and raised back to Flagstaff. This was taken at 5:30 pm, looking at the sunset calculator, twilight wat at 5:42 and sunset was 6:51, and there was no moon until late.


http://www.sunrisesunset.com/mobile/day.asp?latitude=35.551855&latitude_n_s=N&longitude=111.6258&longitude_e_w=W&timezone=-7&dayOfMonth=5&month=4&year=2010


(It looks like I took G34 at 6:47pm the next day...really racing the light :-)


The top of SP Crater is 2 miles from the camera location.


location: (35.55186, -111.62589) 

There is more description, links to more of these images, and maps, at http://scaleindependent.com/projects/drats

description:
This is an overview image of the North face of the SP crater with surrounding lava
flows and the west side of the vc1 crater.

The road cut from G6 is near the middle of this image.

We suspect the road cut in G6 represents a different unit which the SP crater

name: Road cut through flow, North Side SP Crater (G6)
description: Detail of road cut through the flow. This looks like an older flow, this image is intended to allow people to explore this flow in detail, and then to see a larger context in gigapans  G7 and G8.

name: Looking north east from SP crater (G5)
description:
The next question is : where can we find evidence of other flows, and make the
genetic link to their souurce cones, and then find a place where one flow
covers another.

There is more description, links to more of these images, and maps, at http://scaleindependent.com/projects/drats

name: Detail of Lava flow on SP Crater (G4)
description:
There are some curved surfaces in this flow.  This was moving slowly, and cooling
at the same time, so the cracks are forming because it is cooling and
it wants to move, so tension is created and cracks form.  This looks like
 a large amount was moving in mass - it doesn't look like a talus pile.

It appears similar across a long range

There are variable sizes in the bolders, but it is very blocky.  So this
isn't the slow ropey thin fast moving lava, it is jagged, and heavy.

There is more description, links to more of these images, and maps, at http://scaleindependent.com/projects/drats

name: Closeup of talus slope on SP (G3)
description:
Showing various sized rocks and variability in vessicularty of rocks.  Basically some rocks have a lot of holes, like scoria, and others don't.

Also showing one piece that looks like an outer part of the flow , and some
pieces showing evidence of welding, with scoria landing on partially cooled
flow.

There is more description, links to more of these images, and maps, at http://scaleindependent.com/projects/drats

name: Looking North at the flow from the flank of SP crater(G2)
description:
Standing on the base of the northern part of the SP crater, facing northward looking at the basaltic flow off of the crater.

The goal of this image is to show the continuity between the flow and the crater.  And the wind is so hard that I have to hold the tripod with one hand, and while I was recording notes my netbook was literally blown out
of my lap and thrown six feet.  The wind is from the west, left in the image.

This flow is continuous with this cone.  You can see no break
between the rock types in the flow and in this cone.  This was not obvious from the map.

The gigapan also show the full extent of the flow.  All of the dark brown outcroppings throughout the
photo appear to be one continuous unit, that can be traced in the photos.

The wind lifted the gigapan a few inches off one leg 2/3rds of the way through the photo.  (there
is a slight artifact on the top of the image, probably from this.

The mounded areas, the outcroppings which stick up in the flat areas are for now of unknown
origin.  We don't know where they belong.  For example, to the right the outcropping just left
of the road.

What we need to do in terms of a working field geologist 'what do I need to figure out"
we know we have an extensive prominent flow unit we can trace.  But we have smaller out
croppings which is something else which is probably volcanic.  We need to see if we can
determine the age relationships between the outcroppings and this prominent flow.

Two things to show:
1. that this flow is continuous with this cone.  You can see no break
between the rock types in the flow and in this cone.  This was not obvious from the map.
(I thought it was not the case).

2. A good aerial of the extent of the flow unit.  you may be able to trace
the boundary of the flow unit as well as we know based on what is uncovered.


There is more description, links to more of these images, and maps, at http://scaleindependent.com/projects/drats

name: Close up on northern flank of SP Crater (G1)
description:
Note surface pattern of cracking on the rocks. Also there is Scoria, a highly vesicular volcanic rock (vesicular means there are many cavities, or vesicules).

This is from the Northern flank of SP Crater, near to where an apparent alluvial layer flaking off of the cone contacts a major flow.

There is more description, links to more of these images, and maps, at http://scaleindependent.com/projects/drats/

name: La Llorona
description: In 2011 there was The Pier, and in 2012 that crew decided that the way to make the pier even more interesting would be to crash a Spanish Galleon right into the end of the pier. So they did, and it was amazing. Here it is shown on Tuesday after the burn, being dismantled. Here is a nice blog post on it: http://blog.burningman.com/2012/09/culture-art-music/pier-2-and-la-llorona

name: ReinCOWnation by the Houston CORE
description:
"ReinCOWnation is an interactive sculpture of a gaunt but strong longhorn bull emerging in triumph from the desert floor. A symbol of fertility to the ancients and a recognized emblem of the Lone Star State, which we Houstonians proudly call home, this classic creature will use his strength in this powerful moment to rise in rebirth out of the playa. And, like Black Rock City itself… to honorably return back home into the dust after his glorious burn, without a trace."

More info at http://bit.ly/reincownation 

URL: http://www.houstoncore.org

name: Paige
description: At Burning Man 2012

name: A riverboat art car
description: These folks were camped 200 feet from us for a week, and somehow I didn't meet them, and I only got a picture at the last minute Tuesday as I was leaving.

name: Greg Junell's Temple, final image
description:
Sitting in the chaos, and flames, and smoke. We had to be wearing 'sensible shoes' in order to carry it on there, and to fill it with wood.

There was a bit of nervousness at the end as we were pouring 4 1/2 gallons of white gas, and two gallons of lamp oil, onto it.

But the work was all worth it. It burned beautifully, and we formed a line holding hands and watched it, and some people sang "The more I give, the more I have," and when the last vertical finally collapsed we had a group hug.

name: Greg Junell's Temple, ready to burn
description: We carried Greg's Temple from the spot past the Temple to the Man burn. You can (mostly) burn what you want there.

description:
Josh Ellingson, Franz Ablinger (monochrom)

The Purgatron is Josh Ellingson and Franz Ablinger's contribution to RoboExotica 2011. Are you dissatisfied with another cocktail robot's creation? Send your bland cocktail out of purgatory with the Purgatron. Insert your drink into the bartender robot and choose the fate of your cocktail's soul. You can take the devilish path of spicy hellfire additives, or enhance your cup with heavenly sweetness. Either way, your drinking experience will be changed for eternity.

description:
Oliver Höftberger, Robert Neuner, Michael Müller

MELMACC (Mysterious Electrical Machine for Automated Cocktail Creation) is a cocktail mixing robot focussing on versatility and reliability. It boasts a wide selection of drinks, which can be ordered via a custom touchscreen interface. MELMACC has been in development as a hobby project since 2003, adding new features and improving existing ones constantly. The latest additions are a robot arm to add a straw and stir your drink and a mobile web interface so you can order a cocktail from your smartphone.

description:
Ursula Bogner, Christopher Eder, Stefan Kaltenegger, Basti Meier, Lukas Nöckler, Rüdiger der Roboter, Daniel Schütter, Daniel Stein

Run for your lives! The Tiki God's wrath is upon us. He stole all the drinks on Hawaii and hid them in his mountain lair. Now he is running amok and only you can save the Hawaiian village from complete destruction. Your only weapon: a blowgun. Shoot his eyes or his mouth, so that he stops rampaging and releases the alcoholic treasure.
PS: Bring a friend, so that he can get the treasure, while you are fighting the God.

description:
>>Karin Frank is able to sculpt feelings. Fellsteiger 1 ("the furclimber", the word being
derived from "mountain climbing") is one of her great works related to phenomenological
philosophy. It is trying to establish an intersubjective expression, showing deep emotions
in form of a woodcarved sculpture.<<

During the opening for Von Robotern, die Bilder bauen at Galerie Lisi Hämmerle. Part of Vienna Art Week 2011. http://viennaartweek.com/2011/events/100/%C2%BBvon-robotern-die-bilder-bauen%C2%AB/

description:
'Winter in the MQ' is a fun event in the courtyard of the MuseumsQuartier. There is a curling rink (field? pitch? what do curlers curl in? I am only used to curling from the command line :-), and an RC car track, and booths 
where  you can get Punsch in convenient mugs.  I like punsch...and I like standing in the chill air looking at the world through a robot, with my punsch on a ledge, the shutter remote in one hand and focusing with the other 
hand.

Taken from the steps of the Leopold museum. Manual exposure, but still some vignetting.  I think because the camera adjusts the white balance. I have raw versions of the source images, and I should check.

name: Niko, at Noisebridge
description: There are a few, er, issues with this portrait...like, for example, the ears. But there is lots of Win as well.  So enjoy the win, ignore the artifacts, or better, recontextualize the artifacts as signifiers of the dynamic nature of personality, they are reminders that Niko can't be captured as one simple presentation of self, that people are more complicated than that, and, er, something else with the ring of erudition.

description:
Specimans from Salmon River
ID 2009, 2 of 3
Assorted Taxa 

f22/125th
Macro just under 1.5x
flash at 1/16th power

--
The green patterns in the background are part of my 'chromakey SIFTable pattern.' The idea was to give the stitcher clues, in the form of 
easy to extract SIFT keys from unique(ish) features to give the stitcher solid control points.

Paul Heckbert suggested 'busy font characters.' 

Then once the image stitches I can (in theory :-) treat those colors like a chromakey/greenscreen and remove the pattern. But in reality, I am 
able to identify the macroinvertebrates even with the background, so perhaps it can stay.  So many interesting questions!

The green patterns in the background are part of my 'chromakey SIFTable pattern.' The idea was to give the stitcher clues, in the form of 
easy to extract SIFT keys from unique(ish) features to give the stitcher solid control points.

Paul Heckbert suggested 'busy font characters.' 

Then once the image stitches I can (in theory :-) treat those colors like a chromakey/greenscreen and remove the pattern. But in reality, I am

b) ?

name: Experiment: Compositing two gigapans
description:
I used the gdal command line tools to resize two gigapans, the outer one of the office, the inner one of the shoe shown in the monitor, then geo reference them to an arbitrary coordinate system, then composite the two images into one image.

In this test I shrank both images (in tif format) to 10% of their original size.

Computer stats: 3892.52 MB RAM, 2 CPUs
Total time 8:07 (12 seconds per picture)
  Alignment: 34 seconds, Projection: 52 seconds, Blending: 6:41
(Preview finished in 2:16)
description:
The Nautilus car is a reimagining of the Jules Verne Nautilus Submarine for the Burning Man festival.

It is covered with delicious details.  When I commented that having a linkage so that the rudder actually turns was 'over the top'
the response was 'as opposed to the rest of the car which is not over the top?'

This was taken the day before the car was intended to be loaded up. Lots of folks were crawling over and into her.

They fitted the harpoon gun while I was there.  A phantasmigal creation which they took care to make sure was properly
counterweighted so that a person could control and aim it. I don't think it 'does' anything, except to delight the senses
and incite the imagination.

I put a Luma Lite flash on the camera.  It will be better to put off camera lights in key points, but the single flash technique worked surprisingly well in this image.  At least, when compared with the nearly unusable ambient light.

description:
This is the old Gasworks in Seattle, on Lake Union.

This facility converted coal into 'City Gas' - which was then piped throughout town.  Gasworks like this used to be a common feature in most cities, but 'Natural Gas,' which we mostly use now, has become so convenient that it drove out the alternatives.

The Gasworks has been turned into a lovely park. While I was there several wedding parties and photographers came to take pictures.

name: Hanger One at Moffet Field gets a face lift
description:
Hanger one is being (de) skinned.  The metal skin is leaching bad stuff which then runs directly into the bay.  The current answer is to strip the metal skin and then put on a temporary cover.

This Gigapan shows a wider view from near the same spot from November 2008.
http://gigapan.org/gigapans/12057/

And here is a stunning collection of historic photos of the construction of Hanger One and of the Macon, which Hanger One was built to hold.
http://www.howtobearetronaut.com/2011/08/construction-of-the-uss-macon-airship-c-1932/

description:
Close up of part of the pool shown in gigapan http://gigapan.org/gigapans/80039/

The Laguna de Santa Rosa is a wonderful landscape.  This spot is about 8 minutes from my house.  There is a highway about 45 feet behind the camera position. 

I think this is a particularly persistent vernal pond - a pond which tries up in the summer.  We are still a bit away from the proper 'creek' channel.  But during the winter this whole area, including the fence posts, can be underwater.  People launch kayaks and canuoes from the little pull out where I have my car!

description:
Three macro views at Steelhead beach on the Russian River.  Taken with the low cost macro rig.

On the left is what is left of moss ? growing on a former gravel screening plant.  The center are plants which where growing right up to the gravel screen plant, and on the right some tenacious plants growing on exposed dirt a 100 yards away.

Several different views of the area are here:
http://gigapan.org/gigapans/tag/steelhead_beach/most_popular/

The left image is from one of the walls of the gravel screening plant-a snapshot of the location is in this gigapan:
http://gigapan.org/gigapans/23131/

I held the wooden frame up on the wall with one hand, and manuvered the camera and snapped photos with the other hand.

The center image location is snapshotted here:
http://gigapan.org/gigapans/23025/

(it is just to the left of the frame in the 'wall' image)

And the right patch of ground is near the parking lot, 100 yards or so from the gravel plant.

Looking at the juxtaposition of these three images, representing 'nano' climates so near to each other makes me reflect on the immense complexity of our systems - both natural and human created systems.

I think about George Washington Carver, who it was said in an area where 'you saw ten plants he saw a hundred' and he held that visible diversity in mind at the same time that he could see the layers of soil two or three feet down, the different layers, the organisms, the structures which hold moisture and nutrients.

And this diversity is exposed at every layer.

name: Meitosis stages in mouse testis
description:
6x4 mosaic, captured with 40x objective on microscope.
Individual frames captured as HD video, split into individual images, and then focus stacked with Zerene stacker.

Approximate scale is 8.7 pixels per micron

I was told that the large structures which are over 100 microns wide, are not the cells.  The cells are much smaller, the 8 micron scale bar is, I believe, bracketing one cell.  Corrections from actual biologists welcome!

description:
Saturday night, April 2, at BarBot 2011. The American analogue of the RoboExotica Show highlighting robots in Cocktail Culture. This year it was held at the new Parisoma work space.

The White Russian Hippee Robot!  Magnus was here in San Francisco this year.

Johannes, and Monochrom, have built a wormhole between San Francisco and Vienna. It is great. Robots and people pass through a semi-porous membrane and arrive on one side or another.

Here is how the robot  looked at Roboexotica Dec 2009
http://gigapan.org/gigapans/38559/

For notes on operation and construction read the snapshot descriptions on this gigapan, the 2009 gigapan, and the gigapan of the rear of the machine http://gigapan.org/gigapans/74406/

(And if I have anything wrong, please add a comment!)


(description copied from the previous version)
I love the little details in this piece. Note the grace notes in the snapshots, such as the auger ice feed, the feedback switch on the ice chute so you only get one cube of ice, the fun servo driven selection indicator from 'milk' to 'vegan.' 

The valves are made with servo motors attached to chopsticks - when the servo is 'off' it turns the chopstick which obstructs the tube with the fluid flow. 

This machine also delivered a quality White Russian. It turns out that I have been using _way_ too much milk in my White Russians. They are tastier this way! 


---- 
Official Description from http://www.roboexotica.org/?robots2009 

White Russian Hippie 
1st Vienna Cocktailrobot Workshop Crew: Heidi Trimmel, Karina Schwarz, Franz Ablinger, Chris Veigl, Magnus Wurzer 
This cocktail robot is the outcome of the first Vienna cocktail robotics workshop held in 2009. As the name suggests, it offers the choice between a White Russian or its vegan counterpart - the White Hippie! Made from Arduino processors and found parts, it mixes and serves the cocktail with ice when a glass is placed on the designated platform and a choice of drink made by pressing a button.

----
My deconstruction...
In terms of Physical Computing and interaction this cocktail robot implements (at least :-)

4 drink selection switches (with four extras on the former elevator pad)

4 servo motor chopstick valves to control proportions of four different liquids

1 servo motor to provide feedback with a pointer whether you are getting a dairy or vegan drink

1 feedback switch to make sure there is a cup in place, so a drink is not wasted onto the floor.

1 DC motor to control the ice augor

1 spinner switch to provide feedback that ice has actually been delivered to the drink.

Possibly a hall effect sensor (or just a magnetic switch :-) to indicate when the ice auger is at the top position, and needs to be reset and refilled with ice.

Total time 9:13 (20 seconds per picture)
  Alignment: 43 seconds, Projection: 1:53, Blending: 6:36
(Preview finished in 4:29)
description:
This is the full Orrery at the Long Now museum.  The last time I shot in the museum I was limited to available light, and so I shot just the base of the Orrery from an angle: http://gigapan.org/gigapans/2770/

Now I have lights (2/17/11), so here I experimented with shooting the full orrery, shooting straight into the window and trusting that I could balance the ambient window light with the light from the flashes.  I had three flashes in this shot, the two main ones on either side, and then a little flash with a purple gell on the base-I am experimenting with the idea of treating curated spaces, like museums, as 'found objects' to be remixed into new compositions solely by the way the spaces are lit.

My experiments on this have not pregressed very far yet :-)  Right now I am at the point of adding a little new color in one spot, but it seems fun.  And short of a tota colorl remix, having control of light and being able to light all of an interior scene so that it gigapans cleanly is a wonderful goal!  I have so many images of interior spaces which, frankly, are just lacking because of the light.

description:
From the Museum of the Long Now: http://longnow.org/
Feb 17, 2011

Other Gigapans of the Long Now Museum, including January 2008:
http://gigapan.org/gigapans/most_popular/?q=long_now

From the text:
"Shows the placement of the Chime Generator inside an underground chamber.

This model represents about 30 vertical feet of what will likely be several hundred feet of vertical shafting leading up to the final Clock chamber.  This vertical shaft will house the large stone weight that will store the energy for the Clock, the chime generator, bells, and speed governing system.  This model shows three sections of what would be ten bell ringing stages. The bells would be hung above the chime generator.

This tunnel will be excavated by a twelve foot diameter raise bore through solid limestone. The stairs would then be cut by a custom robotically controlled diamond toothed belt saw."

Setup shots of how I took this are on flicker:
http://www.flickr.com/search/?s=int&ss=2&w=41715395@N00&q=setup long_now&m=tags

name: Kaiser-Wassser building in the snow
description:
Today was a day for exploration, and adventure. I took random subway trains, and explored new areas. And I didn't take the massive mast.

But when I got here I had to have a shot of the details - the names of rivers pressed into the building, the hammocks, the snow.

So I held the gigapan on a trash can and did my best to cover the lens with my handto protect it from the snow.

Taken Dec 17, 2009, restitched version of
http://gigapan.org/gigapans/39148/

description:
Every time we look more closely we find the unexpected.  It could be water erosion on Mars, traditional foods offered in a market catering to people we think of as the 'enemy,' 
and then we can start looking more closely.

"There is plenty of room at the bottem," said a guileless Feynman.  And as the poem goes
"Big fleas have little fleas,
Upon their backs to bite 'em,
And little fleas have lesser fleas,
and so, ad infinitum."

As for me, I am discovering a deep desire for this work.

name: Aestetix and his face
description:
Aestetix is a nifty guy.  He runs the 'Five Minutes of Fame' event at the Noisebridge Hacker space in San Francisco, and he does lots of fun things.

Whenever I see him he is smiling.  Of course, there is the selection effect that for the most part the only times I see him he also sees me, so the Scientist in me must argue for the null hypothesis that he only smiles when he sees me.

In any event...

description:
I set up a little portrait studio in the back of the Burning Man
Earth booth at SF Decompression, 2010.

The studio is shown here:

images from a Canon T2i - An 18 megapixel Digital SLR. I used the 55mm

description:
Initial focus stacked results from the MicroGigaPan adapter kit.

This was taken with a 10x objective on the National Optical model 163 microscope.

It is 2 columns x 3 rows by 27 focus stacked images per position.

I used Zerene Stacker to create an individual focus stacked image from the 27 images in each position, and then GigaPan Stitch to stitch the resulting 6 images.

name: Portrait of Johannes Grenzfurthner
description:
Johannes is the founder of Monochrom, the group which organized the conference where I discovered studio light.  So he has a lot to answer for :-)

Monochrom has a web page: http://www.monochrom.at/
and Johannes has a wikipedia page: http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Johannes_Grenzfurthner

Is that enough meta data?  Do I need to cut and paste something, or tell amusing anectdotes as well? 

Fine.  His last name, according to him, roughly translates as &#039;johnny ford watcher.&#039;  Someone in his family tree spent his career looking at a bit of water until something of interest occured.  And then this unknown relative sprang into action, welcoming the traveler, or calling out an alarm for dangerous events.

And that reflects, in a neatly wrapped bit of metaphor, what Joahnnes does today.  Watching the river (ie. culture) and creating  events to welcome or warn us about what is happenening out at the Ford where things happen while we sit comfortable in our bathrobes drinking mojitos.
 
If he did not already have a name you could call him Kultur Grenzfurthner, or Cultie to his friends.

And If it were not already named Monochrom you could call the group CultureFordWatcher.

description:
I set up a studio with a seamless white background and professional flash lighting at the Arse Electonika 2010 conference (http://www.monochrom.at/arse-elektronika/)

I spent about $80 renting lights and light stands and maybe $150 buying a wireless flash trigger for the camera, and a 9 foot wide by 35 foot long roll of paper, and maybe something else.

It is amazing how much of a difference, how much better results, you can get from simply using the right equipment!

The conference was Thur and Friday evenings and all day Saturday and Sunday.

It was in a different venue each day, so I had the opportunity to iterate over the process of creating an environment to allow for the capture of high resolution portraits.

I tried to copy images from the camera after each person ( I sometimes succeeded at that!) and I tried to stitch at least some of the images while the event was in progress.

This worked very well!  I was able to get feedback reasonably quickly which allowed me to grow my techniques.  I had some successes, and some failures, and a number of images which seem like 'failures' but which I think just need a few hours of time fine tuning and polishing...

In one case the 'real time' processing allowed me to realize that a particular portrait had completely failed, and I was able to retake the images to capture a portrait of a person who I really wanted to capture.

In 2008 I really had it driven into me how important it was to process (stitch) at least some of the images you take when you are at an event.  I was at Burning Man and spent a day taking images.  I brought the raw images into the stitcher and realized that the camera had missed a number of frames.

I was able to diagnose the problem, and fix it.  I then retook the images which I thought were worth retaking, and then when I was given an opportunity to capture a 'once in a lifetime' (or at least once a year :-) shot I was able to do it with much more confidence that the shot would work out. (this shot in fact: http://gigapan.org/gigapans/8582/)

So this is the third studio setup I created for the conference...in reality this is also the third (people as subjects sized) photo studio I have ever created :-)

description:
My Makerbot arrived, and so I spread out all of the pieces on a table and took 170 pictures of it...what else should one do with a new Makerbot?  

For the uninitialized, the Makerbot is a 3D printer.  A robot that makes things.  You create a model in software, and then send GCode and the Makerbot makes it  See http://makerbot.com

Interstingly and not accidentally, we are using the same GCode interpreter in some of the micro and macro gigapan rigs, and also if you look at a Makerbot through the 
right lens it looks similar to something you might use for a macro or micro gigapan...the whole world of moving (machine tool|camera) under computer control to 
(make|take pictures of) things has much overlap.

My friend asked me what I wanted to make with the Makerbot and my answer was something that looks like a makerbot, or probably a Reprap Mendel (a similar rig) with a camera in place of the extruder head...

description:
The coastal defenses around the Golden Gate are inspirational at multiple levels.  At one level many brave men and women worked hard in order to protect us from foreign enemies.

And they are cool ruins to explore.  But the other lesson is that the darned things were never (I think literally _never_) actually used for our defense.

Either they were a very very good defense against tigers - after all, you don't see any tigert - or they just represent a horrible waste of human effort, ingenuity, and money which could have gone towards better causes!

description:
The Temple of Flux and Temple crew in the valley formed by the Temple.  From 60 feet.  DPW heavy equipment operator Snake Oil took me up in this lift an hour and a half or so after taking me up over 100 feet - higher than the man.

Haggis and I went out to check on the temple, and on Sunday afternoon before opening the Temple Crew was _done building_!

There were not airtools going, no saws, no heavy equipment being frantically driven.  The crew was mostly sitting in the playa cleaning up MOOP ('Matter out of Place' - trash to the rest of us).

On Sunday before opening they were done, and were cleaning up!  This was an amazing feat of organization and of keeping to a schedule!

Often it seems the temple is still under active construction until Wed, the middle of the week of the event!

  Alignment: 2:47, Projection: 3:36, Blending: 24:21
(Preview finished in 13:28)
description:
SYZYGRYD is a two and a half ton musical instrument, an art piece, a Burning Man project, and did I mention that fire is involved?

Lots of fire.  

Quoting from the About page:

----------
Syzygryd is a town square for the collaborative creation of music. It’s a public space, it’s a sculpture, and it’s a professional musical instrument. It’s the most beautiful expression we can imagine of the joy we take in community, music, technology, fire, sculpture and architecture.

Three large custom hardware grid sequencers. Each controls a single instrument and they share a clock, so they’re all synchronized. By controlling time, pitch, and harmony, these devices make it easy for people with no musical training or talent to create melodious compositions.

The consoles are arranged at three equidistant points around a 60′ diameter circle, far enough apart that the participants can see one another but can’t communicate verbally. Each console reflects the state of the others. Three people collaboratively create a continuously evolving piece of music without communicating, except through the music itself.

The center of the circle is a huge metal tornado of cubes, pulsing with synchronized sound, light, and fire.
http://www.syzygryd.com

description:
SIGGRAPH Studio - Wearables and Inflatables.

The inflatables are designed on a computer, and then the pattern is projected onto the wall where the participants cut out plastic material based on the projected pattern.  They then weld the plastic together with heat, with an opening at the bottom where the piece can be fitted onto an air blower so that it can blow about and entertain everyone.  The wearables involve conductive thread and shiny things, and multiple degrees of awesome.

The cameras are all dust coated - including inside the optics and on the sensors, so there is some strong vignetting in the gigapan.  Oh well.  If your camera isn't covered in dust by the end of the even than you probably were not taking very many pictures!

description:
Friday, 09/04, the City is in full bloom.  My city!
This took 46 minutes to capture all of the images. 
 but I'm not sure if this is 8/28, or 9/4...

You can watch sunset catch up, and pass the Gigapan robot, as the robot works one column at a time to catch the light, and the light works one arcsecond at a time to hide from the camera.

The light always wins.

description:
I just hiked along the Esplanade taking Gigapans every 1/2 hour on the clock circle, somtimes more often.

Here I met someone looking for Center Camp - if you are at 2:30 and Esplanade and you are looking for Center Camp than something has gone horribly wrong for you!

And I chatted with an emergency services person with a German accent who was doing a safety walk through.  She talked about being 'chewed out' for her lack of radio discipline by someone from Seattle.  I _suspect_ with no further evidence, that it was our friend Jesse doing the chewing :-)

Taken Sunday 8/30, before the gates opened.

description:
This was the fifth annual Nasa on the Hill event.  A group of people from all over gathered to showcase some of the interesting work which is being done at NASA.

We gathered in the Foyer of the Rayburn house office building.

This Gigapan was taken relatively early in the day.  There was a reception with food in the evening.   The Administrator of NASA, Charles Bolden, spoke, and seven or eight Members of the house were present.

It was all exciting stuff!

stitcher_notes:

Panorama size: 151 megapixels (14724 x 10256 pixels)

  Alignment: 31 seconds, Projection: 20 seconds, Blending: 5:02
(Preview finished in 1:39)
description:
Microscopic Gigapan of six inch ruler.  The numbers mark tenths of an inch.  The hash marks are hundredths of an inch.

This is a five by four image grid, with the XY stage moved manually.

This was taken with the 4x objective, through a 2.5x camera adapter.  Without the camera adapter the Field of View is
4.5 mm, but with the camera adapter the Field of view is approximately 1.8 mm per picture, or 0.07"

The five images across show about 0.24",  or 0.048" per picture after counting for overlap.

The image is 14,724 pixels wide, showing 0.24", or 61,350 pixels per inch, or 2,415 pixels per mm.
(please correct my math if I am wrong!)

Taken with the National Microscope model 163, which we got with generous support from Make Magazine.

This Gigapan is part of the macro/micro/nano GigaPan project, which is working to take large pictures of very small things.

This mosaic consists of 170 images taken at ~1000X magnification.

Read more on the project blog at http://nanogigapan.blogspot.com

And see more of our work on the gigapan site, at the pages of some of the project members:
http://gigapan.org/profiles/mollyg/
http://gigapan.org/profiles/jay/
http://gigapan.org/profiles/rich/
http://gigapan.org/profiles/fourchambers/

description:
View from the top of Crittendon Mound.

This was shot with the Canon T2i set to the 35mm equivalent of 236mm.   I used f11 - According to my references the 
diffraction issue should be minimal at f11, and only possibly noticeable at f16, and I wanted Depth of Field.

It was shot with manual focus, focused just a 'nudge' from infinity.

I can use 1/3 of a second time per picture, which ends up as about 26 pictures per minute.

description:
The back patio, home of countless gasifier experiments, and plants growing in biochar, and home to bbq's, and where Kimric taught my son spencer about flame rockets.

They involve a propane torch and a foot of conduit over the gas outlet.  You slip a 2 liter soda bottle over it, depress the gas for 10 seconds - without a spark - and then spark.

The bottle demonstrates the process of thermal expansion, plus it makes noise and is lots of fun.

Jim joked that Spencer owed him a bottle of propane, and as i recall, when he saw one, either at the store or in my shop, he made me bring it to Jim.

It was a glorious time.  Playing with fire and children.

The red shipping container on the right was also the site of my GigaPan Death Leap!

In june 2008, a few months before I started officially working on the GigaPan project I took a gigapan from atop that container of the Gasification workship in progress.  When i tried for a second picture the gigapan lurched somehow, and slipped off of the shipping container and landed camera end first.

There was mechanical carnage and destruction.  Documented in this flickr set
http://www.flickr.com/photos/rich_gibson/sets/72157605626723048/

Here is the last GigaPan I took before the death leap:
http://gigapan.org/viewGigapan.php?id=6060

description:
We slid across this icy walkway to get to the SBahn Station near the Sunflower hostel - or we walked a bit more to the UBahn station
when we went to the Technical Museum, or Checkpoint Charlie.   You can see the 'Suicide Circus' torward the center right, and 
just the experience of snow and trains.

This is apparantly a party neighborhood.  Jay and Molly were told that 'this was the spot' to be a for New Year's Eve, and so they
were on this bridge when all hell erupted.

New Year's Eve in Berlin involves rockets and sparklers and roman candles and big bangs, and little oversight and less control.

The rest of us were at the cBase hacker space, along the river.  That was insane, but mostly there were just the people at the cBase
party shooting off fireworks there.  We could see some others, but we were sort of tucked behind the building along the Spree river, and 
in a bit of a canyon.

So our space was insane with fireworks.  Jay and Molly report that where they were seemed as ground zero to insanity.

Highly recomended, would go again...but maybe wearing safety goggles.

description:
I took Spencer and Maddy to the German Museum of Technology. We got off of the ubahn at the Gleisdreieck station and I looked up and was frozen, jaw dropped, fingers twitching with the need to take a GigaPan of THIS MURAL and to do it RIGHT THIS SECOND.

This was Jan 2nd, saturday, in the afternoon.  The only angle on the mural was from within the DHL parking lot, which was locked, or _maybe_ with the mast from over the fence.

I walked around the whole lot - the kids entertained themselves by throwing snowballs at each other, but I was unable to find 'the one true angle' which would let me capture the mural in the way it called to me to be captured.

It was also cold.  And I didn't have the mast.  And I had two rambunctious excitable kids, and did I mention that it was cold?  So on Jan 5th the kids and I went out and discovered curry wurst - the kids loved that, and even liked french fries with mayonoise, and then headed back and I talked with poor German to a DHL guy who gave me permission and I captured this GigaPan

description:
Another in the series of 'Great Reveals' from train stations.

You come up the escalater and face this massive victorian building which was a gas storage tank from the 
city Gas Works.


And there is a whole row of 'crazy' green apartment buildings next to the four gas towers.

The towers are now redeveloped, see the wikipedia link:
http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gasometer,_Vienna

And see this link
http://www.wiener-gasometer.at/en/

This is high on my list of awesome city 'reveals' = those places where you come out of the ground, or around a corner, and suddenly your breath is taken away.

Coming out of the Roman Subway at the Colisium stop is another classic, and the Stephanplatz station here in Vienna.

And perhaps every subway stop, every ascent from the underworld, provides the opportunity for the grand reveal.  Symbolically, and literally, we are exiting the underworld, escaping the lost place below and entering again the land of light and warmth.

We are Persephone coming up in the spring to bring warmth and fertility, or perhaps, just coming up into the warmth and fertility.

description:
The Flower Building 38-40 Linke Wienzeile

Yesterday, Dec 24th, we went shopping at the Naschmarkt.  And my eye was stuck by these incredible buildings, and I had to GigaPan them, RIGHT NOW, even though I didn't have my mast or a tripod.  But there was a handy electrical transformer, so I made an effort by balancing the Gigapan on the surface.

It turns out that they are already pretty famous without my GigaPanning.  "Majolikahaus and Medallion House' "Designed by Otto Wagner, the genius of the Viennese Jugenstill.  The facade of the Majolikahaus, entirely covered in tiles, is an explosion of pink and green ceramic floral designs.  Gilded medallions depict female forms on the walls of the adjacent apartment block (1898)."

It was here that Rose said to me that it was important that "you confess to yourself that you are madly in love with Vienna."

I wake up and if the sun is up I sort of panic, I have an urgency to be out there.  Not sure where, or doing what.  Taking GigaPans and experiencing the city.  Rose says 
"I mostly think that it is just you wanting to take pictures of your beloved."

description:
I stumbled on the  one day, and it was awesome!   Exploring a city is filled with connetions.  Where you realize that the 'Naschmarkt' that everyone tells you to go to is really the same place you had been going to - you just didn't realize the name.

Stumbling onto Karlsplatz via Ubahn was one of these experiences for me.  First, the station is great fun.   It is massive, because there are entrances at each of a number of far corners.

The exit towards Karlsplatz is like an underground garage.  You could drive cars right now a ramp and into the station.  You stroll up and you are in a gorgeous park and in the middle of Vienna.

I love the Ubahn in part for the dramatic reveals that you get.  The best is coming up the Stephansplatz exit.  You ride up an escalator, and then look up, straight up, and the spires of St Stephans reveal themselves, at first high, impossible high and then you ascend to street level and it is still massive, but then there are some near human features.  You can see the door, and the ground for reference and you are not lost entirely within the heavens.

Donauinsel has another reveal.  Especially in the whispy snow and grey sky which i had.  You glide to a stop, get off the train, and then you are in the MIDDLE OF THE RIVER!  Really.


There is a fantastic park, a great Christmas Market, the Naschmarkt and Seccession are across the street

description:
The Poster For Tomorrow project organized events in 24 locations world wide in order to celebrate Global Human Rights day, 12/10/2009.

http://www.posterfortomorrow.org/pages/view/the_pencil_is_mightier_a_glance

I have several other gigapans of the event, search 'posterfortomorrow' or click this link:

http://gigapan.org/gigapans/tag/posterfortomorrow/most_popular/
--
This is what I originally wrote.  I did not know what the exhibit was, I only knew that suddenly posters had erupted outside of the former Hapsburg Imperial Stables.  Posters advocating freedom and human rights.

It was just another wonder in a city of wonders.  Levels of symbolism on top of other layers.  The chain link fence panels are the same thing used to create 'free speech' zones in America.  

The posters, well the posters scream.  

There are 4 or 5 triangle fences, like this one, with ten posters on each side.  There are some duplicates I think, 
but so so many.  Design will save the world stuff.  I like it.  I have three more captured at night, I like the effect,
but I can't take the freaking cold...doing 4 second exposures with a 2 second self timer to allow the camera to
settle, and triggering and advancing each shot manually because I can't reliably get the time per pic, so I can't leave the GigaPan alone to just capture the images.  It was so cold.

I got three or four gigapans, and then I went back - it seemed like I went back just a day or two later, and the posters were already gone.

It was a transient experience.  Here, gone, done.  I just wandered out, as I did, and there was an exhibit.  I didn't know that 12/10 was Global Human Rights day, and then I captured images, according to the camera time, which I didn't reset for Vienna Time, it was 9:30 pm or so of Global Human Rights Day.

description:
At the corner.  This is a great European spot.  You come up past St. Stephens, on Rotenturmstrasse, and you can get great books just off Rotenturmstrasse on Wollzeite at Wawowa books, or coffee and amazing treats at the corner of the next street, Lichtensteig at Zanoni and Zanoni, or turn right and go to Figlmüller for amazing Schnitzel on Backerstrsse.  The schnitzel is literally bigger than your plate - you should get the small, half portion, even if the waiter jokes with you that you are a child, and then you go one more block to Fleischmarkt.

And there is a full size spider man, on the west side, clinging to the wall above the comics shop.  And this tiny little street curves ahead of you, Rabensteigasse, and to the left is a movie theatre, Cine Center with movies like 'Kapitalismus cheri' and 'die papstin antichrist' and 'plastic planet das weisse band' and things which drive me back 
to my German texts.

Striving for meaning in the words, and meaning from the architecture and the layouts of the streets and the patterns of people.

But the key is to head away from Grabon and the gaudyness of Karntner Strasse, and up, and then maybe turn left on Feleischmarkt and right, and around to St. Ruperts, Ruperechskirche, the oldest church in Vienna, and it looks a bit like a garden shed when compared with Stephansdom, but there is a charm to it.

And maybe drop down the stairs, towards the new River Boat dock which is, itself, architected to look like a river boat, and across the bridge and down to the path along the donaukanal, and stroll for miles.  literally miles with a legal grafitti wall on your side.  And the cold swift Donaukanal to the other side.

That is vienna.

description:
There is only now, this minute.  I set up the gigapan and went for breakfast.  I assumed I would take several, many, gigapans of the monochrom office.  But so far, this is the only one.  And it didn't complete - it was supposed to be a 360, and the color balance seems off, and some images were missed.

And I messaged Johannes that I was taking a GigaPan of the office.  He said, no, wait, it is so messy from RoboExotica, wait until we tidy up.  And I responded with a definition to the word 'Tidy'

-----
Tidy is a special word we use to describe a space which on Earth has achieved the perfection we imagine for Heaven. When we tell ourselves to wait, to not show something until it is tidy we are taking onto ourselves the role of ultimate control, the power of creating the  order reserved for processing souls to their eternal reward and damnation. 

We are all sinners to the false god of Tidy.

Notes: this does not apply to the actions of roommates in a shared kitchen.  In that case it is acceptable to demand that those filthy slobs should get their shit together, show a little respect, and tidy up.

description:
'The Imperial Cabinet of Natural Curiosities' you could call it.

Old things in antique wooden cabinets.  No fancy remounting here.  If they have a Tyrannosaurus rex here I am sure it is still mounted upright, on legs and tail!

(though they do have a copy of Dippy, the Diplloducus donated to them by Andrew Carnegie in 1909.

The museum opened in 1889.  Putting it on par with construction of the Carnegie Museum of Natural History, in Pittsburgh, which opened in 1896.

There are striking parallels between the monumental architecture along the Ring road in Vienna and the buildings in Pittsburgh.

It was a common era, and there were similar aspirations whether in the West or in old Europe.

The KML link is now entertainingly close
http://gigapan.com/gigapans/39267.kml

description:
(Description from Rick Steves http://www.ricksteves.com/plan/destinations/austria/viennamonument.htm)

"The Monument Against War and Fascism consists of four thought-provoking statues.

The split white monument, The Gates of Violence, remembers victims of all wars and violence, including the 1938–1945 Nazi rule of Austria. Standing directly in front of it, you're at the gates of a concentration camp. Step into a montage of wartime images: clubs and WWI gas masks, a dying woman birthing a future soldier, and chained slave laborers sitting on a pedestal of granite cut from the infamous quarry at Mauthausen Concentration Camp.

The hunched-over figure on the ground behind is a Jew forced to wash anti-Nazi graffiti off a street with a toothbrush. The statue with its head buried in the stone (Orpheus entering the underworld) reminds Austrians of the consequences of not keeping their government on track. Behind that, the 1945 declaration of Austria's second republic — with human rights built into it — is cut into the stone. The experience gains emotional impact when you realize this monument stands on the spot where several hundred people were buried alive when the cellar they were hiding in was demolished in a WWII bombing attack."

description:
SantaCon Vienna occured.  It was pretty awesome.

Here the Santas are gathered in the courtyard of the Museumsquartier having Punsch.

Things were peaceful until the Santa's started sliding on heir bellies down the Curling Pitch, competing for distance.  They were asked to stop, and then the Curling Attendant cleaned up the ice with his shovel.

Then there were carols, including the classic "I wish I could see you naked," to the tune of 'We wish you a Merry Christmas."   After a fairly patient interval things decayed, a little bit.  I started to set up for a GigaPan from the platform of the curling pitch and an official told me not to.

"This area is not for singing.  Who is the leader of this group."
"Well, " I started, "There is no leader."

He had different ideas and found a leader and encouraged a peaceful Santa Exodus.

Some people, including families with small children, seemed delighted by it all, and others were horrified at some of the antics and some of the lyrics.  There was a lot of excitement and pictures taken and happiness and then there was definitely a bit of unhappiness mixed with the shock, joy, and amazement.



From Wikipedia http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SantaCon
"SantaCon is a mass gathering of people dressed in their various interpretations of Santa Claus costumes and performing publicly on streets and in bars in cities around the world. The focus is on spontaneity, creativity, and the improvisational nature of human interaction while having a good time and spreading cheer and goodwill to all they come across."

(Preview finished in 3:29)
description:
The Poster For Tomorrow project organized events in 24 locations world wide in order to celebrate Global Human Rights day, 12/10/2009.

http://www.posterfortomorrow.org/pages/view/the_pencil_is_mightier_a_glance

I have several other gigapans of the event, search 'posterfortomorrow' or click this link:

http://gigapan.org/gigapans/tag/posterfortomorrow/most_popular/
--
This is what I originally wrote.  I did not know what the exhibit was, I only knew that suddenly posters had erupted outside of the former Hapsburg Imperial Stables.  Posters advocating freedom and human rights.

It was just another wonder in a city of wonders.  Levels of symbolism on top of other layers.  The chain link fence panels are the same thing used to create 'free speech' zones in America.  

The posters, well the posters scream.  

There are 4 or 5 triangle fences, like this one, with ten posters on each side.  There are some duplicates I think, 
but so so many.  Design will save the world stuff.  I like it.  I have three more captured at night, I like the effect,
but I can't take the freaking cold...doing 4 second exposures with a 2 second self timer to allow the camera to
settle, and triggering and advancing each shot manually because I can't reliably get the time per pic, so I can't leave the GigaPan alone to just capture the images.  It was so cold.

I got three or four gigapans, and then I went back - it seemed like I went back just a day or two later, and the posters were already gone.

It was a transient experience.  Here, gone, done.  I just wandered out, as I did, and there was an exhibit.  I didn't know that 12/10 was Global Human Rights day, and then I captured images, according to the camera time, which I didn't reset for Vienna Time, it was 9:30 pm or so of Global Human Rights Day.

description:
Some German words are easier to understand than others.

Kultur Fur Alle, or Culture For All, is easy to translate.  And it is inspiring.

It is deeply inspiring to be here in the Museumsquartier.  There is so much 
happening, so many interesting people creating and curating and studying
art and culture and social movements.

I love it.

But German is not yet my total friend!  I happen to now know that an Ausfahrt is just an exit.  But for a long time, even now, I see a word which is to fart as au juice is to 'juice.'

description:
from http://www.roboexotica.org/?robots2009

The Miraculous Stigmatabot
David Fine, Aaron Muszalski, Jonathan Moore; Crew: David Rappoport Allen, Ted Beatie, John Daniel, Jonathan Foote, Jason Dusek, Tom Raphael Eaves, Valerie Leavy, Audrey Penven, Shalaco Sching, Ramon Yvarra (a special thanks to Noisebridge, a collaborative DIY hackspace in San Francisco, CA)
A forgotten relic of a fictional future, The Miraculous Stigmatabot was rebooted for your sins. A martyr to the abandoned ideal of the Anthropomorphic Automaton, the Stigmatabot now performs the miracle of transubstantiation, using what little energy flows through his fading circuitry to convert water into wine. Penitents are invited to place their grail below the Stigmatabot's wound spigot (lower right torso) and then pour pure water into the Stigmatabot's holy funnel (upper left shoulder), while repeating the following prayer:
O Roomba, O microwave,
I forsake thee!
Gloria in anthropos deus.
In Nomine Asimov, et ASIMO
et Robosapien,
Amen!

description:
Description from http://www.roboexotica.org/?robots2009

The Corpse Reviver
Benjamin Cowden
This mechanical contraption produces a death-defying cocktail by means of levers, knobs, and a bit of sinister alchemy. The victim/operator must place their glass in the center of the device, and then pull each of four levers in turn. The first three levers tip the bottles, pouring the ingredients into a holding container on top of the machine. The fourth lever releases the contents of the beverage, which gurgle down a tube and spray over ice, finally funneling into the user's cup. The apparatus is entirely mechanical, and its outcome (a mixture of brandy, cognac, and sweet vermouth) positively malignant.

description:
update: new annotated gigapans of this robot, see:
http://gigapan.org/gigapans/tag/whiterussianhippie/most_popular/

I love the little details in this piece.  Note the grace notes in the snapshots, such as the auger ice feed, the feedback switch on the ice chute so you only get one cube of ice, the fun servo driven selection indicator from 'milk' to 'vegan.'

The valves are made with servo motors attached to chopsticks - when the servo is 'off' it turns the chopstick which obstructs the tube with the fluid flow.

This machine also delivered a quality White Russian.  It turns out that I have been using _way_ too much milk in my White Russians.  They are tastier this way!


----
Official Description from http://www.roboexotica.org/?robots2009

White Russian Hippie
1st Vienna Cocktailrobot Workshop Crew: Heidi Trimmel, Karina Schwarz, Franz Ablinger, Chris Veigl, Magnus Wurzer
This cocktail robot is the outcome of the first Vienna cocktail robotics workshop held in 2009. As the name suggests, it offers the choice between a White Russian or its vegan counterpart - the White Hippie! Made from Arduino processors and found parts, it mixes and serves the cocktail with ice when a glass is placed on the designated platform and a choice of drink made by pressing a button.

Hobot

Stefan Arztmann und Matthias Esterl
Poor Hobot has two big problems: He is addicted to booze and broke as hell. Of course the second problem is the reason for the first and vice versa. It is not easy for him to see his mechanical colleagues giving alcohol to foreigners all day long. Please help this homeless, homesick fellow by sparing him some change. He would be pleased if you could help him get shit-faced for another day. Thank you!

description:
Julius Steinhauser tends the Plasmastaub. You arm wrestle the metal hand in front of him, and assuming you are successful a glass drops down, the whiskey bottle tips up to fill it, and a rubber band loaded arm sends the drink shooting down the bar along with a musical 'Yee Ha' They got an award for Cocktail Serving. It was an awesome experience! I'd like to figure out a way to get them to Burning Man. Wouldn't it be awesome to put this bar at the front of the Burning Man Earth camp? "I'm sorry, I was busy downing a shot, how do I load my GPS with the Black Rock City Map?"
Here is their full description, from the http://www.roboexotica.org/?robots2009 page. Plasmastaub Robot design by: Deutschmann, Clifford; Figo, Jakob; Jimenez-Arango, Carolina; Puffing, Michael; Sadilek, Helene; Steinhauser, Julius -- Robot environment by: Holzer, Florian; Kollmann, Lisa; Maurer, Franziska; Wieshofer, Ursula

After the robot exterminations of the year 3078, PLASMASTAUB is one of the last remaining jackBOTS®. A group of resistance fighters managed to retrieve him and some of his fellows from neuralization by sending them on an adventurous journey through time. The robots were supposed to be sent into the future where a harmonic coexistence between man and machine would be possible ... but something went terribly wrong. Due to a loose lead in the energy supply module of the time machine, the target date was reset to January 1st 1970.

In the year 2009 a group of FH-students decided to submit a wild-west-bar-robot to ROBOEXOTICA. Looking for the required materials they found the (albeit severely damaged) jackBOT® in the very back of one of their grandfathers' metal dumps. Immediately becoming fond of the robot, the project team decided to repair PLASMASTAUB. By changing a few parameters they turned him into a rude, arm wrestling, whiskey slinging wild-west-barBOT.

description:
The trains to New Jersey are fast and comfortable.  The only problem is that the schedules, especially on the weekend, are a little sparse.

No real problem if you plan ahead, but a little dicey for casual use - especially if, like Rose, you are used to the Subway which runs more or  less 'all of the time.' 

Being constrained by a train schedule is stressful - not having the option to finish something because the next train is not for an hour is a bit stressful.  OTOH, the trains are clean, and comfortable, and fast, and cheap - $8 for an offpeak round trip.

description:
An ad hoc crossing of Stevens Creek in Mountain View.   I used to live a couple of blocks from Steven's creek, in Los Altos, and there was a half mile section of the creek which I knew well.  They would put up a temporar, but high, earth damn at Fremont every year.  The idea was to give the water more time to percolate down to recharge the water table and reverse ground water subsidence issue.

Playing in the creek is a good thing to do.  For kids, and maybe for adults as well.

description:
Mikel Maron, Jay Longson, and I went out nighttime gigapanning.  I was on the sidewalk when a car zipped by, going really really fast.  Jay says a police car was right behind, but somehow I missed that.  It was such a socially transgressive behavior that I just stared with my mouth open as the driver ran the red light a block up, and then swerved to run the next red light, but was hit in the rear quarter panel and spun around.  There was drama in the Streets of San Francisco.

Mikel took his first Gigapan, here:
http://gigapan.org/viewGigapan.php?id=15369

description:
Mikel Maron, Jay Longson, and I went on a night gigapanning safari, getting Mikel ready to gigapan his way through London, ?, and India.  This is from the south west corner of Dolores Park.

I drove past this spot a view months ago and thought 'self, this is a good place for a nigttime gigapan'

I set the camera self timer to two seconds, and the exposure was about 5 seconds.  I put the gigapan into manual mode and hand triggered each shot.

description:
gigapan user Dogshrink found a NASA link describing the wind tunnels here:
http://history.nasa.gov/SP-440/ch6-15.htm

The first diagram is of the "Ames 3.5-foot hypersonic wind tunnel"  and shows the four large vacuum tanks which are just to the left of this, and these high pressure tanks.

Straight back are two more vacuum tanks, the one in front is being dismantled.

To the right is the 'cyrogenic separation tower and auxiliary equipment building'

These are shown in more detail in this gigapan:
http://gigapan.org/viewGigapan.php?id=12016

description:
"So what is in those tanks?"

I asked two different people that question, and got the same response.

"Nothing"

They were literally correct.  To several significant decimal places these tanks contain nothing.  This represents the majority of the nation's strategic vacuum reserve.


vacuum tanks at NASA - AMES.

description:
Taken from the top of a shipping container at the shipyard.

This is the last gigapan I took before the unit took a lens-first death leap from the top of this very shipping container.

focal length 137mm

Pictures of the aftermath of the gigapan death leap here:
http://www.flickr.com/photos/rich_gibson/sets/72157605626723048/

Here is an image of the same location, but from a different angle, taken Jan 16th, 2010 - you can compare what has, and has not, changed at the Shipyard in the intervening 18 months.
http://www.gigapan.org/gigapans/41437/

Here is the GEK assembly room Jan 16th, 2010
http://www.gigapan.org/gigapans/41375/

name: Berkeley, under University Avenue  (bay_day_8)
description:
Night shot under University Ave at 4th Ave, Berkeley.  Brennan's restaurant and bar on the left, Spenger's Grotto on the right.

Used a 3 second self timer, so camera jitter would settle down, and a 1.6 second exposure.  

I (finally!) turned off image stabilization, and I think that makes a different. 

I also spent time fiddling with the white balance.  I had a calibration target with me, but I ended up taking some test shots and looking at them in the display and decided that 'Daylight Fluorescent' was the effect I liked.

 

40 images
f 3.2
1.6 second exposure
iso 200
Focal Length: 59mm 


I put the gigapan into manual mode and hand triggered each exposure.

name: The new Sebastopol Skate Park, under construction - wider view (skate_park_03)
description:
I took three gigapans of the skate park under construction.  I had to slip the gigapan under the fence, and manipulate it by fingertip through the chain link.  Note the f....d lightening on the right as I tried to adjust lighting on the fly so that hte houses would be properly lit.  I'm starting to think that I may simply decide to dislike sky, and find a nice horizon to top of image color gradient, say 5-10 pixels wide, and clone it for all of my skies.  One issue is that lately the clouds have been spectacular.  But in a 'subtle spectacular' way.  I want to keep those clouds.

Or perhaps it is simply a matter of craft.  Craft is not like sincerity.

"Sincerity is the key to life, once you can fake that you can do anything."


focal length: 133

name: Rosie the Riveteer WWII Home Front Memorial (rosie_01)
description:
25% of the shipyard workers on the west coast during WWII were women.

We are still living in the aftereffects of the social changes that came from WWII.  

During WWII the defense department outlawed racial discrimination in defense contractors.  We didn't have that in the civilian world for a long time.

The post war dislocation was massive, and there was a passive, perhaps even active, attempt to push the lessons learned back into the memory hole.

It was okay for Rosie to rivet and weld and lay plate during the war, but she was supposed to quietly go back home and bake cookies once the men came home.

It almost worked.  But people don't forget.

I told him to just sit down, ride the current down, and I'd catch him.  "Trust me" I told him.  finally he did.  I said "i'm so proud of you, you trusted me!"  he replied, "no, actually

description:
Spencer and I rented a car...well, I did the renting, and drove up to the Iao Valley.  The rental car lady said her grandfather lived in the area and told her stories of the river running red once a year with the blood of the ancient warriors, and the Night Marchers thundered through at night.

"Were you scared?" spencer asked.  "No, when you live in Hawaii you get used to that sort of thing."

name: Iao Needle (l41)
description:
The Iao Valley, and the Iao Needle, the formation on the left, are simply awesome!

This is my first 'real' gigapan with the Canon S5IS.  I didn't really take much advantage of that big lens in this one.  I was experimenting with varying the focal length by image. Sure, it would be nice to take everything at 426mm...but that would be more or less insane!

This was at 130mm, and I had the white balance set to Tungsten, hence the blue tinge.


I smashed my gps in the rental car door, so the Iao Valley gigapan locations are a little approximate...something hard for my hyperfocused self to allow :-)

description: I finally figured out how to stittch and upload from the command line, so I didn't see this before I uploaded it.  But I 'm glad I didn't.  I am starting to like these massive 'wrong turns' in the stitching.  The 'messed up' stitches create something new, something I would not think about.  they invert and twist reality in systematic ways, they are not the twisted vision of a Dali, but something where reality is both source and subject of the tools.

description:
The back of the Geffrye's is to the right, past that overpass.

The 'overpass' is a train track, soon to be a new line on the, er, non-underground.

This is just a random street scene in London.  Off in the up and coming neighborhood of Islington/Shoreditch, but really for all of that just a place.  A normal sort of 'stuff goes on and people chop wood and carry water' sort of  neighborhood.

name: Motorcycles on a London Street (b7)
description:
I sat on the curb, pausing the camera, reshooting images marred by passing cars and people, and still didn't get it perfect...

This was one of my earlier efforts, and this row of bikes just called out 'take me'

I took so many gigapans of 'trivial' subjects, 'missing' out on the significant sites.  I never did make it to the British Museum, or the Tate, or any museum other than the Geffrye's.  I did go into Saint Paul's, and while it is pretty cool, it didn't move me at a deep level.  Don't take me for a philistine, the memorials in the basement and the tombs are moving, and the whole 'if you would see his monument look around you' Christopher Wren thing is cool - it is like this ancient structure which is not in any way lost in antiquity.  'Everything' (for some values of everything) is known about St. Paul's.  It has a continuous history, and it is all glorious, but it wasn't a part of my history, unlike the damn fruit flies which are currently bedeviling my efforts to type and to drink my White Russian (dude!).  Don't let your children make candle holders out of apples, and if they do, for god's sake, don't let them get 'lost' in their room for a week!

name: All Soul's Church, Hursley, Keble Grave (a4)
description:
Around this time in my gigapan 'career' I got to wondering what would happen if you took gigapan closeups.   The answer is, well, you get a closeup...

On the left is the grave of the poet John Keble, after whom Keble College, Oxford, was names in 1870.

See http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/John_Keble

name: Canal, London, UK (d1a)
description:
A virtual copy of another of my images.  I was attempting to do something towards high dynamic range (hdr) imagery.
The other image has a grey dreary sky, which wasn't literally the case when I took my images...but at a larger level, it _might_ have been the statement I was interested in making.
Hard to tell, really.  I am now gearing up (largely 'mentally') to be able to kick these panos up a level.  To do things like preprocess the soure images for saturation and white balance and whatever else matters.  But since it takes so long to take and stitch even a conventional gigapan I just don't know...

name: St Anne's Church, London, UK (b1)
description:
I took the train up from Hursley, than I might have planned, and then wandered about with my heavy back.  I took refuge at St. Anne's and took panoramas.

This is the same church shown in http://gigapan.org/viewGigapan.php?id=693, but from the other side of the rear yard.  I almost didn't upload this second one, but looking closely there are interesting features (revealed in the snapshots) in each view which are not visible in the other image.
What we clearly 'need' is a device which continuously records gigapixel panoramas wherever we go...obviously that is technologically absurd, except, doing just a little extrapolation on current trends we should be able to have that capacity within a surprisingly small number of years.

This image took about 7 minutes to take, and maybe 4-5 hours to stitch.

name: All Souls Churchyard, Hursley, UK (a6)
description: I love this attenuated light.  Everything from the churchyard feels ephemeral, the way a church yard should feel.  Meanwhile, there were thousands of boy scounts somewhere in England on some big jamboree, and floods not that far from us were wiping out things.

name: All Saints Churchyard, Hursley, UK (a2)
description:
Grave stones planted by the back wall of All Saint's church.

I don't _think_ that those are the actual graves-it has the look of the stones having been moved back out of the way to protect them, to preserve them, something.  Or maybe those are not markers, but just memorials.  

It reminded me of the basement of St. Peter's - there are rooms with gates over the doors and fragments of engraved stones mounted on the walls, and sitting on the floors.  They are what is left of the graves of the venerated from 'way back.'

150 years in the Hursley churchyard is old, with decaying stones, but 400 years in a room in St. Peter's basement is as nothing.

name: Castle Crag and Trees (l7)
description: The view point for Castle Crag is lovely.  Just far enough from the car park that you can no longer see or smell the cars, with the crags in front, shasta to the right, other ranges to the left, forest behind...crisp fall air, it was divine.

name: Castle Crag State Park, California (l8)
description: from the view point of Castle Crag state park.

name: Ponds at sunset, and Interstate 5 (l14)
description: Pond at sunset, I5 in background

name: Orland tower, and Ice Box Market, Orland California (l13)
description:
We discovered Orland on our drive to my Uncle's funeral in Yoncalla in August of 2004.

The Ice Box appears like a conventional convenience store, but something was different.

They have a sandwich counter, and the sandwiches were amazing.  Huge sandwiches, inexpensive, they hit the spot.

This last trip I stopped in.  The old owners were not there.  A new person took over about six months ago.  I asked if the sandwiches were still good.  

"Let me put it this way, when we started we were selling four or five sandwiches a day, now we do 80."

name: Lake Shasta Marina 1, at the bottom of the stairs (l11)
description:
A low lake shasta from down on the banks.

The ground was littered with dead and open freshwater clams.  Put not your faith into a constant water level in lake shasta...

name: Laguna de Santa Rosa from Occidental Road bridge (j3)
Cinder cone and Mount Shasta (l6)
description: Mount Shasta straight ahead, the cinder cone to the left.

name: I5 from a hill...(l3)
description:
I headed out from Ashland, and made it just past 'the highest point on I5,' Siskiyou Summit, at  4,310 feet.

I felt the pano-urges come on.   I blame gigapan for turning a six hour drive into a 10 1/2 hour drive...

name: Columbia River from Rainier, Oregon (l1)
description:
The Columbia River from a beach in Rainier Oregon.

Forest products processing plants visible straight ahead, and to the left.

name: Yoncalla Pioneer Cemetary - overview (k4)
description: View of the Yoncalla Pioneer Cemetary from near to the entrance.  The miller plot is on the left center, then two applegate plots to the right.

name: Lake Shasta Marina 1, from the top of the stairs (l10)
description:
Lake Shasta Marina 1, and I5 Shasta Bridge on the right.

I have crossed that bridge many times.  Dozens of times in my life.  But I had never stopped, exited, poked around.  

The key to travel, the key I always forget, is that you have to STOP.

name: Castle Crags State Park and Ammiratis Market, California (l9)
description:
I blasted down I5, glanced over and saw these awesome rocks...big rocks, Crags in fact.  I decided, too late, that I should photograph them.  I went to the next exit, a ways down the mountain, turned around, came back.

I didn't even know there was a 'Castle Crags State Park.'  It is gorgeous.  I have other gigapans from up closer, from the 'vista point' but then I came down and stopped at Ammirati's market.  And looking around, and into the beer cooler, I found satisfaction.

It wasn't that I wanted Beer, but I did buy a bottle of 'Ammirati's Castle Crags Ale,' but rather, the whole experience.  At the end of my 11 day trip, while heading home, I finally had that feeling I was looking for.  That feeling of just bopping around, taking pictures, experiencing life.

name: Dick and John Gale Graves at Yoncalla Cemetery, Yoncalla Oregon (k3)
description: The grave stones of my Father, Uncle, and Great Grandfather.

name: Overview of Miller family plot, Yoncalla Pioneer Cemetery, Yoncalla Oregon (k2)
description:
The Miller family plot in the Yoncalla pioneer cemetery.

My great grandfather's oldest brother was born 1/15/1874 and died 15 days later.  When he died my GGGrandfather and Grandmother bought 'The Miller Plot' in the Yoncalla Pioneer cemetery.   It is one plot aways from the one where Charles Applegate is buried with his wife (who is my ggg or so Aunt).

This one was too small to upload :-( so I got around that by stitching two 360 degree panoramas together.  I think I have three 360's of this scene...maybe I should do a 1080 degree pano :-)
